I finally got around to calculating the Church leaving rates from the latest Pew Religious Landscape Survey. The PRLS is one of the few surveys that has questions about both former and current religious affiliation with a large enough sample size that it can tell us something about Latter-day Saints.

So what do we find? According to the 2023-2024 PRLS, 54% of people who were raised Latter-day Saint still identify as such. So about half.

However, when we run the same numbers for 2014 it was 64%. And when we run the same numbers for the 2007 wave it was 70%.* So it looks like we’ve had a decent drop-off in the past twenty years. That means for every ten people who were raised LDS about three would have left in 2007 world, and five would have left in 2024 world, so two more out of ten (or 1.5 out of ten depending on how you’re rounding). This isn’t insignificant (but, to be fair, neither is it the “nobody used to leave now everybody is leaving” story that some observe). So the anecdotes and general vibes people have been getting of more people leaving the Church do have some bearing in the data.

You assume that the "Stick of Judah" is the Bible, while the "Stick of Joseph" is the Book of Mormon. In the latter days, the two have been joined together, forming the bulk of Mormon scripture. Mormonism's literal interpretation of scripture does not extend to hundreds of passages it rejects as corrupted or that it skews to suit its own purposes. In the case of Ezekiel 37, Mormons not only neglect the plain sense of the words, but also ignore there true interpretation, given by God-in the very same chapter. First, the Hebrew term translated as "stick" (aits) is never use anywhere in the Old Testament to mean "Book," "scroll", "writing", or anything similar. It is variously translated as "wood", or "branch", "timber", or "tree."
Needless to say, the Book of Mormon allegedly written on metal plates, not scrolls or sticks. 
Second, the correct interpretation of this symbolic action of the prophet is given just a few verses later. Ezekiel is to take the two sticks, put them end to end and hold the joined ends in his hand. He thus displays to the people a "single" stick, once again united. This is to show that the scattered remnants of the southern kingdom ("Judah ") and northern kingdom of Israel ("Joseph") will be returned from exile, restored to their land, and made one nation again.

The strongest population contrast is this: the Book of Mormon text implies societies that could include tens of thousands to hundreds of thousands in major polities like Zarahemla, while the precontact Baja Peninsula is usually described as having small, sparse, mostly foraging populations rather than large cities; the Yucatán Peninsula, by contrast, was home to dense Maya civilization with major cities and very large regional populations.
Several demographic readings of the Book of Mormon infer a large population base. One recent analysis estimates about 300,000 people in Zarahemla in 87 B.C., growing to 450,000 in Mormon’s day under moderate assumptions, with armies such as 40,000 and 30,000 men being plausible within that framework. Other Book of Mormon population discussions also place Zarahemla in the 80,000 to 150,000 range before the Amlicite war, depending on assumptions.
The precontact Baja Peninsula is not usually described as supporting urban, metropolitan societies on the scale implied by those Book of Mormon population estimates. Historical summaries of indigenous Baja describe small, dispersed groups living by hunting, fishing, and gathering, and note that contact-era populations were relatively sparse; one source even characterizes the indigenous population as “extremely primitive forms of life and culture” in the sense of lacking large complex urban systems. That does not mean Baja was empty, but it does mean the usual archaeological picture is a low-density peninsula, not a landscape of major city-states.

The Yucatán Peninsula is a much better match for large, complex, urbanized populations. Britannica notes that Maya civilization had more than 40 cities, each with populations between 5,000 and 50,000, and other research places the broader Maya region at millions of people at its peak. Recent estimates for the northern Maya lowlands also describe very substantial densities, on the order of 15 to 19 people per square kilometer in some reconstructions

The Arroyos in the desert were made by flash floods. The water that flows after a rainstorm is quite impressive. I watched one such flash flood roll 300+ lb rocks and entire trees downstream. A pile of dead people would be no match for a flash flood.  It's also possible to damm up smaller aroyos to make a reservoir this water may not last long, but it's a common practice in the southwest. I believe that retaining ponds in the sagebrush steppe of wyoming are also dependent on precipitation. Its not that hard for me to imagine such retaining reservoirs would have been utilized to capture water during monsoon season. Ancient Israelites were know for storing rainwater in cisterns. 

As far a a day and a half journey across the narrow neck of land. A relatively fit person can walk 40 miles in that timeframe.  I could probably  make that trip in the desert IF there wasn't any significant obstacles.  I can climb mountains no problem, and make good time on a well maintained trail. But t if I had to cross dense shrubby forests, Swamp, Jungles, or  Boulder fields, I'm not doing 40 miles in 2 days. That's more like a week. The narrowest part of Florida is 100 miles. The Panama Canal is 50 miles, and they both have similar problems. There's lots of water and dense vegetation.  No way such a journey could be made in less than 2 days. Now imagine trying to make a defense position. If a lamanite wanted to cross into the land northward, he has the cover of the jungle. However, in baja California, scouting positions could be set up on the tops of hills and mouthains. Trespassers would be seen from a great distance.

Going to play devil’s advocate here, but I think the case against the Jesuits is weak.

They may not have had a detailed survey of Baja at first, but they knew it sat in a comparable latitude band, and they left fairly meticulous records of transporting and transplanting crops. They also would not have needed to carry seeds all the way from Europe to Baja in one unbroken trip. By then, the Spanish already had agriculture established in New Spain, including eastern regions like Florida and Texas, so Baja could have been supplied through existing colonial networks.

Archaeobotany seems to show that the crop lineages now in Baja go back roughly 300 to 400 years, even if the deeper origin of the species is ultimately Middle Eastern or Mediterranean.

So the theory can only really be saved by saying the original Nephite crops died out with their civilization, and then the Spanish later introduced and planted the same kinds of crops about a thousand years afterward, which became the ancestors of what is grown there now. That is plausible, and there is even some symbolic power in it, but it is not actual evidential support for the Baja Book of Mormon theory.

Loved this episode and I respect the text-based approach, but I did some digging on two of the key claims:

**The "people of Laman" claim:** The Jesuit records (via Pablo Martinez's *History of Lower California*) don't actually record the natives calling *themselves* "the people of Laman." What's documented is that one tribe (the Guaycuras) used the word "laymón/laimon" as their name *for another tribe* (the Cochimíes). It's also the name of the Cochimí language family. Interesting? Yes. But it's not self-identification, and the similarity to "Laman" may be coincidental.

**The seed DNA study:** The University of Arizona researcher is Gary Paul Nabhan, and his study's actual conclusion is that olives, dates, pomegranates, grapes, and figs arrived in Baja California via **Jesuit missionaries (1697–1768)** — who brought them from Spain for religious purposes (wine for communion, palm fronds for Palm Sunday). The study explicitly credits the Jesuits. The "curiosity" about overlap with Egypt and Oman oases is about global oasis agriculture patterns, not an unexplained ancient origin.

The seed adaptation argument and the latitude/climate/peninsula case are genuinely interesting. But these two supporting claims don't hold up to scrutiny. Would love to see the sources addressed directly.

Big Picture: What Baja Does Not Show

Unlike mainland Mexico (e.g., Teotihuacan or Tenochtitlan):

No large cities
No pyramids or monumental architecture
No agriculture-based state societies

Why:
The peninsula’s arid climate and isolation favored small, mobile groups rather than dense urban development.

Bottom Line

The Baja peninsula’s “early civilization” is defined by:

Longevity (human presence for ~10,000 years)
Adaptation (desert + marine survival strategies)
Symbolism (some of North America’s most impressive prehistoric art)

It represents a non-urban pathway of human development—arguably just as sophisticated in ecological knowledge and symbolic culture as larger civilizations.

Fascinating theory and very compelling.  I had AI analyze the text of the Book of Mormon and this video and have the following issues that I'd love to be addressed in a follow up video:
1. The "Sea North" Contradiction
The Video's Claim: David Rosenvall argues that the Book of Mormon lands exist on a vertical peninsula (Baja California). He states that they are bounded by a "Sea East" and a "Sea West" and a limited south, pointing out that there is "no land westward or eastward anywhere in the text" (28:50) and that the Nephites are on an "isle of the sea" meaning a peninsula with nowhere to go but north.
The Book of Mormon Text: The peninsula model fundamentally fails to account for a northern sea. The text explicitly states that as the Nephites expanded, they were surrounded by seas on all four sides.

Helaman 11:20: "...they did multiply and spread, and did go forth from the land southward to the land northward, and did spread insomuch that they began to cover the face of the whole earth, from the sea south to the sea north, from the sea west to the sea east."
A peninsula, by definition, attaches to a larger landmass at its base. The Baja California model lacks a "sea north," which contradicts Helaman's explicit description of their geographical boundaries.

2. The "Progressive Seed Adaptation" Theory
The Video's Claim: To explain why an essentially 125-day journey took 8 years, Rosenvall posits that Lehi’s family stopped for long periods to engage in "progressive seed adaptation" (1:06:27). He claims that every time the text says they "pitched their tents," they were stopping for seasons/years to plant seeds, harvest the best ones, and adapt them to new climates and daylight lengths so they would grow in the New World.
The Book of Mormon Text: The text directly contradicts the idea that they were participating in agriculture during the 8-year journey, attributing their survival exclusively to hunting, raw meat, and direct divine intervention.

1 Nephi 16:15: "We did go forth into the wilderness to slay food for our families."

1 Nephi 17:2: "And so great were the blessings of the Lord upon us, that while we did live upon raw meat in the wilderness, our women did give plenty of suck for their children..."

1 Nephi 17:12: The Lord explains exactly how He is feeding them, requiring no agriculture: "For the Lord had not hitherto suffered that we should make much fire... I will make thy food become sweet, that ye cook it not."
The text gives no indication of the labor-intensive process of planting crops, waiting for them to grow, and harvesting them in the Arabian wilderness.

3. The Origin of the Successful Seeds
The Video's Claim: Following the "seed adaptation" theory, the video heavily implies that the seeds that grew "exceedingly" in the promised land were the genetically adapted remainder of seeds gathered along their journey (pointing to 1 Nephi 8:1 where they gather seeds in the Valley of Lemuel).
The Book of Mormon Text: When Nephi describes the successful planting of crops in the New World, he explicitly credits the original seeds from their home, not adapted seeds gathered along the journey.

1 Nephi 18:24: "And it came to pass that we did begin to till the earth, and we began to plant seeds; yea, we did put all our seeds into the earth, which we had brought from the land of Jerusalem."

4. "They Only Go North"
The Video's Claim: Rosenvall points out that because they are on a peninsula bounded by an ocean to the south, the Nephites "only go north" (32:44), constantly pushing northward and hemming the Lamanites in on the south.
The Book of Mormon Text: While northward expansion is a major theme, the text does not support the claim that movements were strictly northward without southward travel or eastward/westward expansion.

Southward Travel: In Mosiah 8:7-8, Limhi sends an expedition of 43 men to find Zarahemla. They miss it, travel far into the land northward (Desolation), and then return south back to the land of Nephi. Furthermore, multiple missionary journeys and military campaigns feature Nephites traveling south from Zarahemla back to the Land of Nephi (e.g., the sons of Mosiah in Alma 17).

East/West Expansion: While Rosenvall claims there is no "land eastward," Alma 50:25 notes that they built the city of Moroni "in the land... which was by the sea east." In Alma 22:27, the Lamanites are described as living in regions bordering the sea "on the east and on the west," showing horizontal lateral spread, not just vertical northern movement.

5. Large Bodies of Water in the Land Northward
The Video's Claim: The entire setting is on the Baja California peninsula, with the "land northward" pushing up into what is currently the Baja/Southern California region.
The Book of Mormon Text: The geographic description of the "land northward" requires immense amounts of fresh water, which creates a textual friction with the deeply arid realities of the Baja/Southern California model.

Helaman 3:4: "And they did travel to an exceedingly great distance, insomuch that they came to large bodies of water and many rivers."
If the geographical model is being evaluated strictly on its ability to match the text, the land northward must feature "many rivers" and "large bodies of water," which is highly incongruent with the geography directly north of the Baja peninsula.

Summary of Video Content: Book of Mormon Geography and Scriptural Digitization Journey

This video presents an in-depth discussion with David Rosenball, a pioneer in digitizing the Book of Mormon scriptures and a proponent of a new geographical model for the Book of Mormon’s physical setting. The conversation covers the origins and development of digital scripture projects, the scientific approach to Book of Mormon geography, and compelling evidence supporting the Baja California Peninsula as the likely location of the Book of Mormon events.

Key Highlights and Insights

1. Digital Scripture Project and Early Innovations

In 1987, David’s father began digitizing the scriptures using early HyperCard software on a Mac, creating the first searchable electronic version of the Book of Mormon.

This project took three years to complete and included detailed footnotes and cross-references.

In 2000, the LDS Church contacted them to help launch the first online scriptures (scriptures.lds.org).

The Rosenballs were the first church service missionaries, assisting in putting scriptures online in 21 languages, including Chinese, which was critical for church access in restricted regions.

Their digitized and reformatted Book of Mormon was eventually self-published in 2017 as the "blue book," presenting the text organized by events, speakers, locations, and times rather than traditional chapters and verses.

The book has sold over 100,000 copies mainly through Costco, priced at $15 to remain accessible.

An audio dramatization of the blue book was produced and integrated into the LDS Gospel Library app in 2024.

2. New Geographical Model for the Book of Mormon

Both David and his geographer father independently arrived at the conclusion that the Book of Mormon events took place in the Baja California Peninsula region, a theory distinct from the traditional Heartland or Mesoamerican models.

Key textual evidence includes:

The directional orientation in the text emphasizes only northward and southward lands, with seas on the east and west sides, indicating a peninsula geography.

The phrase "isle of the sea" (an island or peninsula) and references to a small neck of land separating northern and southern lands support this configuration.

The Nephites’ journey lasted eight years, which is far longer than the traveling distance would require, implying extended stops.

Repeated mentions of "pitching tents" suggest long-term encampments rather than brief stops.

The text mentions that seeds were brought from Jerusalem and planted, with crops growing exceedingly well, which suggests a climate similar to their original home.

3. Seed Adaptation and Climate Matching

The model emphasizes progressive seed adaptation, where seeds brought from the Middle East were planted progressively along the migration route in similar climates and day-length zones to ensure successful growth.

Seeds must be moved only within similar climates and latitudes to thrive due to factors like temperature, soil, and daylight length.

The Baja California Peninsula lies on approximately the 23rd parallel north, matching the latitude of southern Israel and surrounding regions, which supports seed viability.

Scientific studies from the University of Arizona found DNA evidence of Old World perennial crops (olives, dates, pomegranates, figs, grapes) in oases of Baja California, which is unexpected under conventional historical explanations.

Jesuit missionaries, who arrived centuries later, did not introduce these crops, as records show these agricultural features pre-existed their arrival.

4. Geographical and Textual Correlations

Book of Mormon ReferenceGeographical/Textual InsightInterpretationLand northward and southwardVertical orientation, no land east or westwardPeninsula with seas east and westIsle of the sea (1 Nephi)Peninsula or islandBaja California PeninsulaPitching tents repeatedlyLong-term encampments, not transient stopsExtended stays for seed planting and adaptationSeeds brought from JerusalemCrops must be from similar climate zonesSeed adaptation strategySmall neck of land (Alma 22:32)Narrow land bridge connecting north and southBaja’s narrow isthmus

5. Scientific and Archaeological Perspectives

The approach contrasts with traditional models that rely heavily on archaeology and cultural geography.

Rosenball’s team applies physical geography and scientific methods to test textual claims with climate, seed biology, and travel distances.

They have identified over 100 detailed assertions about the geography, none disproven to date.

The Baja model explains the directional movements (always heading north), natural barriers (oceans to the south), and agricultural feasibility.

6. Future Plans and Resources

The team is developing an interactive website hosting the blue book in digital and audio formats for free public access.

They plan to conduct field research and documentary filming on-site in Baja California, including exploring the narrow neck of land.

Early Jesuit journals and local indigenous names such as "Lammon" in Baja California align strikingly with Book of Mormon tribal names, a discovery largely overlooked since the 1960s.

The project encourages open-minded exploration of new evidence and aims to complement ongoing Book of Mormon scholarship.

Core Concepts and Definitions

TermDefinitionLeonaAn ancient navigational instrument described in the Book of Mormon, functioning as a compass with written instructionsPitching TentsBiblical phrase indicating encampment; in this context, suggests long-term settlements for seed plantingProgressive Seed AdaptationAgricultural method moving seeds gradually through similar climates to ensure successful growth and adaptationIsle of the SeaRefers to a peninsula or island, emphasizing the geographic environment of the Book of Mormon landsCardinal DirectionsThe ancient understanding of north, south, east, and west; in the text, emphasis is on north-south orientation

Key Takeaways

The Baja California Peninsula offers a compelling geographical fit for the Book of Mormon narrative, supported by textual, climatic, botanical, and archaeological evidence.

The digitization and reformatting of the Book of Mormon text by the Rosenballs has enabled new ways to study scripture with enhanced clarity on speakers, locations, and events.

The seed adaptation theory explains how crops brought from Jerusalem could thrive in the New World by moving through similar climate zones, supported by modern DNA seed studies.

There is independent scientific validation from non-LDS geographers regarding the presence of Old World crops in Baja California.

The approach prioritizes scientific, text-based analysis over cultural or anthropological assumptions common in other geographical models.

Ongoing research, fieldwork, and digital tools will continue to enrich understanding and accessibility of these insights.

Conclusion

This video reveals the story behind the earliest digital Book of Mormon scripture project and introduces a scientifically grounded geographical proposal that places the Book of Mormon lands in Baja California. By combining scripture study, geography, seed biology, and DNA evidence, the presenters offer a fresh, evidence-based perspective that challenges mainstream views and invites further exploration. Their work highlights the importance of integrating scripture, science, and history to deepen understanding of sacred texts and their physical context.

For further study and to explore the blue book project, visit studythescriptures.com and watch for upcoming episodes documenting their Baja California explorations.

The Baja Peninsula is generally considered low-habitability due to its harsh, arid climate and limited resources. While coastal areas like Cabo and La Paz are populated tourist hubs now, the vast central region is a sparsely populated, undeveloped desert to this day. Living there requires significant adaptation to heat, drought, and no ruins of past large communities.
The region is predominantly arid, with extremely hot, dry summers and mild winters, plus hurricane risks in the south.
Water scarcity is a significant issue, often requiring inhabitants to rely on deep wells or water delivery not ever discussed in the Book of Mórmon.
No record exists of large, dense urban communities in the Baja California peninsula between 600 B.C. and 400 A.D. Archaeological evidence indicates the region was inhabited by highly small mobile hunter-gatherer bands only. These groups, including ancestors of the Cochimí and Yuman peoples, practiced nomadic lifestyles, relying on marine resources, small game, and seasonal desert foods.

Since you have no problem discussing all sorts of mutually exclusive theories of Book of Mormon geography, it might help your audience to discuss the ways in which competing paradigms are evaluated in science.  That is, if you want to go out exploring and come back home safely, it helps to have a not only a compass and a map, but also a legend, and some sense of how and why different maps are made, and when and why they are used for specific situations. Thomas Kuhn's The Structure of Scientific Revolutions describes the difference between evaluating a problem within a particular paradigm, and evaluating different paradigms.   The crucial thing is to have a way of weighing and evaluating that is not completely paradigm-dependent, not completely self-referential.  Kuhn identified the most important criteria as puzzle definition and testability, accuracy of key predictions, comprehensiveness and coherence, fruitfulness, simplicity and aesthetics, and future promise.   Less relevant but common criteria for many people in such debates are things like nationalism, the influence of a favored teacher or familiar tradition (Have any of the rulers or of the Pharisees believed on him?), and rhetorical manipulation, self interest, popularity, and such.   Some self-awareness of the assumptions underlying one's own paradigm amounts to checking one's own eye for beams in order to see clearly.   Consider, Bacon, the Father of the Scientific Method.

"Bacon, the philosopher of science, was, quite consistently, an enemy of the Copernican hypothesis. Don’t theorize, he said, but open your eyes and observe without prejudice, and you cannot doubt that the Sun moves and that the earth is at rest."

One cannot say he is illogical or irrational.  But because he is not willing to theorize enough to consider the implications of his own unconscious assumptions, he turns out to be sadly mistaken.  He could have asked, with Galileo, "What would things look like to an observer on a rotating earth that orbited a sun?   And might that explain planetary motion better than epicycles?"

Many years ago I noticed that Alma 32 offers essentially the same criteria as Kuhn for the same kind of faith decision, where we must settle for progressive "cause to believe" rather than absolute and final "knowing."  Puzzle definition and Testability, an experiment upon the word, mind expanding enlightenment. fruitfulness, deliciousness, and future promise.  Asking "Why us?" whatever one's starting point, involves a very different sort of test than dismissing an opponent for being "Not us."

I spent a good few hours digging into the crop claims in this video. From what I can find, this checks out, I was skeptical because trade at the time of the Jesuit missionaries who are the cited procurers of the seed stock for those Oases should have theoretically allowed for admixture of Egyptian and Omanian varieties and cultivars. However, their records do not give an indication of how, since all of their seed stock came from Iberia or mainland Mexico, which itself sourced its seeds from places other than Oman and Egypt. There are holes, however, as no genetic analysis I could find assessed the genetic background of those same crops in mainland Mexico. Additionally, there have been no genetic clock studies, that I can find, which have been done on the Oases populations or on mainland Old World crop populations. They are assumed to have come from Spanish colonial contact in their entirety. The bigger issue is that there are no reports from these missionaries that there was an endemic population of any of these crops already there, which would have been necessary to point towards a pre 17th century introduction. Interesting, but with many, many caveats and gaps. The observation is indeed strange, but there are other explanations besides the Book of Mormon migrations.

San Diego History Center
San Diego History Center
 +1
However, based on archaeological, ecological, and anthropological studies, the following information can be inferred:
Population Density and Lifestyle: At 1 AD, the peninsula was inhabited by seminomadic groups (ancestors of the Pericú, Guaycura, and Cochimí) who thrived as hunter-gatherers. The population was largely concentrated on the coast and in areas with reliable water, with a much lower density in the arid interior.
Cultural Trends: The inhabitants adapted to a Neolithic lifestyle, focusing on hunting, fishing, and gathering without agricultural development.
Regional Trends: While some archaeological evidence suggests regional population increases, other periods show fluctuations related to environmental challenges, such as a potential decline in lagoon resources around 1000 B.C. or during the Medieval Climatic Anomaly.
Pre-Contact Context: The population of Baja California was relatively small, with estimates of the entire peninsula's native population at the time of Spanish contact (late 17th-18th century) being significantly lower than that of Alta California, likely numbering in the tens of thousands rather than hundreds of thousands.

Brigham Young, who began keeping a regular journal in early 1839, recorded that on 2 July 1839 "Brothers Joseph, Hyrum and others came over the river to Montrose, and went out on the prairie and looked out the sight for a city for the Saints, which was called Zarahemla."[5] Elias Smith, a cousin of Joseph Smith, recorded in his journal for 24 June 1839 the following: "Moved from Commerce to Lee County, Iowa Territory, and went on the farm bought of F. P. Blevins." 87 In his journal for 16 August 1840, he recorded the death of the Prophet's brother Don Carlos and noted that there was a "Conference at Zarahemla" on that day. "[6] These early references to the name of the Iowa settlement previous to March 1841 indicate that the Saints referred to it as Zarahemla long before the reve­lation in question. There is no indication in these early sources that this designation was based upon revelation or even that it was Joseph Smith's idea. This evidence suggests, rather, that the name did not originate with the March 1841 revelation and that the Lord was referencing a location already known among the Saints by that name. The purpose of the revelation was most likely to counsel the Saints to gather at the appointed place and not, as the authors [of the Heartland theory] suggest, to reveal the ancient location of a Book of Mormon city. The Saints did what they would often do—name places they lived after places mentioned in the Bible and the Book of Mormon. There is no compelling reason to associate the Iowa settlement with ancient Zarahemla.[7]"
Matthew Roper, "Joseph Smith, Revelation, and Book of Mormon Geography (A review of "Prophecies and Promises: The Book of Mormon and the United States of America" by: Bruce H. Porter and Rod L. Meldrum)," FARMS Review 22/2 (2010): 15–85

Over the last couple of decades numerous critics of the Book of Mormon have utilized DNA studies to volley attacks, in addition there has been much debate among members of the church regarding the geography or location where Book of Mormon events actually took place.

A few observations on this, starting with the text itself.

Nowhere is the Land of Many Waters described as being north of Desolation. Look at the text closely—once the narrative crosses northward into Desolation, directional markers disappear. That's the only time in the entire record where that happens. Throughout the southern and central lands, you get consistent orientation: northward, southward, sea east, sea west. Cross into Desolation and it stops. Many Waters was southeast of where they crossed into Desolation, not north of it.

And while we're on directions… "west sea," "east sea," and every other sea reference in the text are lowercase. Not one of them is a proper noun. They aren't names; they're descriptors relative to the author's position, and that position shifts throughout the record. This is why every geographic model that tries to pin "the west sea" to one specific body of water ends up twisted in knots. Stop thinking geography first. Start thinking English 101 through the lens of an ancient Hebrew civilization.

On planting and seeds—one important correction first. The latitude-matching argument in this video keys off Bountiful in Oman (~23°N) as the baseline for where the seeds were last adapted. But that contradicts what the text actually says. 1 Nephi 18:24 is explicit: "we did put all our seeds into the earth, which we _had brought from the land of Jerusalem."_ Not from Oman. Not from Bountiful. From Jerusalem. Yes, 1 Nephi 8:1 describes gathering seeds in the valley of Lemuel, and the progressive replanting argument along the Arabian journey is reasonable—but those seeds originated from the family's home stores in the land of Jerusalem, in the inheritance of Manasseh north of the city, roughly 31–33°N latitude. Eight years of stopover adaptation along the Arabian Peninsula would have shifted their tolerance somewhat, but the fundamental genetic programming of those cultivars—photoperiod responses, dormancy triggers, temperature thresholds—was set by centuries of cultivation in the Judean hills, not by a few seasons in Oman.

That distinction matters because it changes which destination latitude should produce "grow exceedingly" results. Jerusalem sits at 31.8°N. Crystal River, Florida sits at 28.9°N—roughly three degrees off. The southern tip of Baja sits at 22.9°N—nearly nine degrees off Jerusalem's latitude. If the seeds are genetically Jerusalem seeds, the destination that best matches their deep programming is the one closest to Jerusalem's latitude, not Oman's.

Now, Rosenball is right about climate zones, and Wisconsin is genuinely bizarre in what it can grow (I say that because I live in the middle of the state). But that isn't the same situation as Florida circa 600 BC. Recent paleoclimate research (Shuman and Stefanescu, 2025) documents that eastern North America was experiencing measurable cooling during the Book of Mormon period—approximately 1.0°C below modern normals in the Crystal River vicinity at the time of Lehite arrival. That's a substantial shift. It means slightly more winter chill hours, reduced tropical disease pressure, and modestly drier conditions—all of which would actually improve the growing environment for Levantine crops compared to *modern* Florida.

More importantly, 20-30 miles inland from Crystal River, you hit the Brooksville Ridge system… well-drained sandy loam soils on elevated limestone ridges with a completely different agricultural profile from the coastal flatlands. Wheat, barley, figs, and pomegranates are all viable there. Even olives and grapes become plausible on well-drained ridge sites under the slightly cooler 600 BC climate regime, where reduced humidity would suppress the fungal diseases and insect vectors that make those crops difficult in modern subtropical Florida.

As they move north into the Tennessee River valley, the Levantine agricultural system comes fully into its own. Winter wheat and barley thrive. Grapes become viable with reduced Pierce's disease pressure at that latitude. Olives survive on south-facing slopes above the valley floor. Figs and pomegranates produce abundantly. The further north they go into Iowa, the better the grain production gets… Iowa's soils are among the most fertile on the planet. The only crop that drops out at northern latitudes is olives, and that's resolved by the Hopewell trade networks, which are archaeologically documented as moving high-value materials hundreds of miles along exactly these river corridors. You don't need to grow olives in the capital. You need to grow them somewhere within the civilization's economic reach and move the oil north by river.

The text says the seeds "did grow exceedingly; wherefore, we were blessed in abundance." That language describes a population astonished by the productivity of their new land—and a Judean farming family accustomed to thin, rocky, rain-dependent soils discovering the fertile, well-watered bottomlands of the American interior would indeed find the results astonishing. You can't get this in a desert coastline. Remember, think English 101 through the lens of an ancient Hebrew civilization first, then look for what follows.
